coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as charge trap elements for nonvolatile flash memory
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Sarkar, Kalyan Jyoti
Sarkar, K.
Pal, B.
Banerji, P.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we investigated the charge storage properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by embedding them between poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) sheets as control and tunneling dielectric layers in a silicon metal-oxide-semiconductor-based nonvolatile memory device (Ag-Pd/PMMA/GQDs/PMMA/Si). The conduction mechanism through the gate dielectric stack embedded with GQDs could be explained by Fowler Nordheim (F–N) tunneling. The GQDs trapped electrons to yield a maximum memory window of ∼5.45 V (at ± 7 V) with a low leakage current density. A control experiment without GQDs obtained very low hysteresis (∼7 mV) even when the gate voltage was swept from +8.5 V to –8.5 V. Thus, the memory window obtained in the present study originated from the GQDs and not from the trapped states in the PMMA/Si interface. The maximum charge density was determined as 12.1 × 1011 cm−2, which is comparable to or even better than that found in conventional semiconductor devices. The endurance and charge retention characteristics were examined to assess the reliability of device operation when the devices were put under a constant stress of ±12 V. The flat-band voltage shifts were determined as 0.16 V and 0.23 V during the programming and erasing operations, respectively, and the charge loss was only ∼14.9% even after 105 s, thereby demonstrating the excellent charge confinement property of the GQDs.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the GQD NVM device.
(a) Cross-sectional TEM image of the device structure. (b) TEM image of GQDs. (c) Histogram showing the size distribution of the GQDs.
J–E plots for the GQD NVM device where the inset indicates Fowler-Nordheim tunneling.
CV characteristics of the GQD-embedded NVM device.
CV characteristics of the control sample (without GQDs).
Variations in the electron density with the gate voltage. The inset shows the changes in the memory window with the gate voltage.
Energy band diagram for the GQD NVM device: (a) under positive gate bias and (b) under negative gate bias.
Charge retention and flat band voltage characteristics of the GQD NVM devices.
Endurance characteristics of the GQD NVM devices during P/E operations.
Variations in the memory window and electron density for different sweeping gate voltages.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as charge trap elements for nonvolatile flash memory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Technology Development Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we investigated the charge storage properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by embedding them between poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) sheets as control and tunneling dielectric layers in a silicon metal-oxide-semiconductor-based nonvolatile memory device (Ag-Pd/PMMA/GQDs/PMMA/Si). The conduction mechanism through the gate dielectric stack embedded with GQDs could be explained by Fowler Nordheim (F–N) tunneling. The GQDs trapped electrons to yield a maximum memory window of ∼5.45 V (at ± 7 V) with a low leakage current density. A control experiment without GQDs obtained very low hysteresis (∼7 mV) even when the gate voltage was swept from +8.5 V to –8.5 V. Thus, the memory window obtained in the present study originated from the GQDs and not from the trapped states in the PMMA/Si interface. The maximum charge density was determined as 12.1 × 1011 cm−2, which is comparable to or even better than that found in conventional semiconductor devices. The endurance and charge retention characteristics were examined to assess the reliability of device operation when the devices were put under a constant stress of ±12 V. The flat-band voltage shifts were determined as 0.16 V and 0.23 V during the programming and erasing operations, respectively, and the charge loss was only ∼14.9% even after 105 s, thereby demonstrating the excellent charge confinement property of the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Charge trap memory is one of the most promising flash memory technologies for use in next generation electronic devices [1,2]. This type of memory has been used widely in electronic gadgets because of its high performance and low power dissipation. A charge trap flash memory device should be characterized by low power consumption, high retention, and endurance properties [3,4]. It can also be electrically programmed and erased in the system after applying an appropriate bias. Different materials such as nanoparticles, graphene, graphene oxide, and polymer electrets have been used as the charge trapping element in flash memories [5–8]. Essentially, the charges are stored in spatially discrete trap states of the charge trapping element, which makes them suitable for memory applications.

In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in studies of graphene because of its unique properties, such as its planar structure, high transparency, large surface area, high mechanical stability, and high electrical conductivity [9–11]. Devices based on graphene have been used widely as sensors, field effect transistors, solar cells, and in nonvolatile memory (NVM) applications [12–15]. Graphene is suitable for applications in different devices due to its advantages such as high mobility, high density of states, low dimensionality, and high work function [16,17]. Recently, tremendous progress has been made in NVM applications. Several studies have reported the development of high performance NVM devices using graphene and its derivatives, e.g., graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide, as a charge trapping medium [18–22]. However, local defects in the tunneling oxide facilitate the leakage of trap charges into the graphene layer via lateral charge movement because the graphene trap layer is a single continuous material [23], which leads to poor data retention and stability. In order to overcome lateral charge movement, devices have been produced with discrete charge trap materials. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used widely as discrete charge trap nodes. GQDs prevent lateral charge movement in devices and lower the sensitivity to local defects, thereby enhancing the data retention properties of devices. Moreover, compared with two-dimensional materials, GQDs exhibit strong quantum confinement [24]. Kim et al. demonstrated the charge trapping behavior of GQDs with an indium zinc oxide semiconductor channel layer on Si as the substrate [25]. Kou et al. reported a method for synthesizing GQDs for flexible NVM applications [26]. Mihalache et al. demonstrated the charge storage behavior of a hybrid nanocomposite made from GQDs and poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG600) [27]. Joo et al. described the size-dependent charge trapping memory behavior of quantum dots with three different sizes and determined a maximum memory window of 8 V at a sweep of ±10 V [28]. The maximum electron density was reported as 2.6 × 10−11 cm−2. Further improvements are needed in the structure of devices to increase the charge carrier density with a higher trapping capacity.

In order to fabricate devices, GQDs are usually sandwiched between two dielectric layers, which can store and release charges after applying an appropriate bias. The different device structures reported previously based on tunneling and control oxide materials are shown in Table 1 . However, the device structures in Table 1 are not cost effective in terms of device fabrication. The device structure comprising SiO2 as a tunnel and a control oxide is grown using the ion-beam sputtering technique, and it requires high temperature processing [28]. The Al2O3 tunneling layer grown by atomic layer deposition is not cost effective and its processing is also time consuming [25]. Recently, poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) has been used widely as a dielectric material because of its unique thermal, mechanical, and physical properties. PMMA can be prepared simply via a solution processing method and readily deposited by spin coating over substrates, thereby reducing the cost of device fabrication. Ooi et al. developed a resistive switching memory by using multiple stacked PMMA dielectric layers on an indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrate [31]. It has also been reported that graphene attaches well to PMMA during device fabrication [32]. Therefore, a thin film of GQDs will form a very good attachment with PMMA.

In the present study, we investigated the charge trapping behavior of GQDs by using PMMA as both the tunneling and control oxide over a p-Si substrate for memory applications in the capacitive mode. The transport mechanism in NVM devices was investigated based on electrical measurements. The memory behavior was studied based on the capacitance–voltage (C–V) characteristics. A key aim of this study was to obtain devices with good retention properties, so we investigated the retention and endurance properties of the device. An energy band diagram was used to understand the trapping behavior of GQDs in the proposed structure (Ag-Pd/PMMA/GQDs/PMMA/p-Si).

First, the p-Si substrate was cleaned in the following manner. The wafer was ultrasonicated in trichloroethylene, acetone, and propanol (isopropyl alcohol) separately at room temperature, with each for 10 min, in order to remove contamination in the form of organic grease. The degreased silicon substrate was then heated in boiling piranha solution (3:1 (v/v) H2SO4:H2O2) and finally in radio corporation of America (RCA) solution (1:1:5 (v/v/v) NH4OH/H2O2/H2O) with each for 15 min. Subsequently, the silicon substrate was rinsed several times with deionized water. PMMA (Sigma-Aldrich) solution was prepared by dissolving in anisole as a solvent. This solution was then spin coated on the cleaned Si substrate at 4000 rpm for 60 s to obtain a uniform coating, before annealing at 130 °C for 15 min. Next, carboxylated GQDs at a concentration of 1 mg/mL in water (ACS Materials, USA) were spin coated on the PMMA/p-Si substrates. To obtain thicker layers, the GQDs were deposited two to three times on the PMMA/p-Si. The sample was then annealed for 15 min at 70 °C to cure the GQD layer. Finally, the control layer of PMMA was deposited by spin coating, before annealing at 130 °C for 20 min. The PMMA was spin coated three to four times to produce a thicker layer. To fabricate the device, silver-palladium (Ag-Pd) was deposited as the top contact and aluminum (Al) as the back contact. In a control experiment, we prepared another set of samples where the device structure comprised Ag-Pd/PMMA/PMMA/p-Si/Al but no GQDs were used.

The GQDs on the tunneling oxide layer were characterized using a Carl Zeiss Supra 40 field effect scanning electron microscope. The thicknesses of the quantum dot layer, tunneling oxide, and control oxide were measured using a cross sectional transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL). The electrical properties of the memory devices were studied using a Keithley semiconductor parameter analyzer (4200-SCS).

The structure of a typical GQD NVM device is shown in Fig. 1 , where the GQDs were sandwiched between the control and the tunneling PMMA. The cross-sectional TEM image in Fig. 2 (a) shows an array of GQDs between two oxide layers. The thicknesses of the tunneling and control PMMA were 15 nm and 40 nm, respectively. Fig. 2(b) shows a scanning electron microscope image of the GQDs. The average size of the GQDs was 10–12 nm and their distribution is shown in Fig. 2(c). Current–voltage (I–V) measurements were performed to examine the conduction mechanism present in our device. The current density was 1.1 mA/cm2 at 1.0 MV/cm, as shown in Fig. 3 . The conduction mechanism through the gate dielectric stack embedded with GQDs could be explained by Fowler–Nordheim (F–N) tunneling.

The current density (J) can be expressed as [33]:
(1)
J
F − N
=
q 2
E 2
16
π 2
ħ
∅
e f f
e x p
(
− 4
ε 0
2
m
e f f
3 q ħ E
(
∅
e f f
−
E 0
)
3 / 2
)
where q is the electronic charge, E is the applied electric field,
∅
e f f
is the effective barrier height of the injective interface,
m
e f f
is the effective mass,
ε 0
is the dielectric constant of the gate oxide,
E 0
is the ground state energy, and ħ is Planck's constant. The inset in Fig. 3 is a plot of ln(J/E2) vs (1/E) in the high electric field regions (1.4–1.6 MV/cm), which shows that there was a good linear fit, thereby confirming that the conduction mechanism was governed by the F–N tunneling mechanism in our device. F–N tunneling occurs when the applied electric field is sufficiently large so the electron wave function penetrates through the triangular potential barrier into the conduction band of the dielectric.

The memory behavior of the metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) devices was investigated based on high-frequency C–V measurements. Figs. 4 and 5
show the memory behavior of the GQD embedded devices and the control samples, respectively, under different gate bias levels. When a gate voltage is swept between the accumulation and inversion regimes, repetitive hysteresis is observed and this is an indicator of the memory effect in a MOS capacitor. Fig. 4 shows the hysteresis loops for the memory capacitors produced by sweeping the gate voltage from ±3 V to ±7 V at 500 KHz. The direction of the hysteresis loops was counter-clockwise, which indicates that electron injection occurred from the substrate to the GQDs.

The MOS structure without GQDs exhibited very low hysteresis (∼7 mV) even when the gate voltage was swept from +8.5 V to –8.5 V, thereby indicating that the memory window was produced by charge trapping and detrapping from the GQDs to the Si substrate.

The variations in the stored electron density and memory window as the gate sweeping voltage increased are shown in Fig. 6 . The memory window was very small at low programming/erasing (P/E) gate voltages and it started increasing above ±2 V. Finally, a maximum memory window of 5.54 V was obtained at a sweeping voltage of ±7 V. In the high electric field region, the maximum memory window tended to saturate, which indicates that the current through the tunneling oxide to the GQDs was equivalent to the leakage current from the GQDs to the control gate via the blocking oxide.

The amount of charge stored in the GQDs can be found using the following relationship [27]:
(2)
Q = C Δ
V
M W
where C is the capacitance density and ΔVMW is the memory window. The variations in the stored electron density with the gate voltage followed the same pattern as the variations in the memory window with the gate voltage, as also shown by Eq. (2). The corresponding values for the memory width and stored charge density at different sweeping voltages are listed in Table 2 . The maximum charge density was 12.1 × 1011 cm−2, which is comparable to and even better than the values obtained with conventional semiconductors and metal nanocrystals.

The P/E operations of GQD-embedded p-Si MOS NVM devices can be explained based on the energy band diagram. In the programming mode, i.e., when a positive voltage is applied to the gate electrode, the inversion electrons will tunnel via the F–N tunneling process from the p-Si conduction band and reach the energy states formed in the GQD region between the tunneling and control oxide layers. These electrons then become trapped inside the energy states of the GQDs, which can act as charge trapping centers for these NVM devices. Therefore, the probability of tunneling will be lower for these electrons when moving from the trapping centers to the gate electrode through the control oxide layer. In the erasing mode, i.e., when a negative voltage is applied to the gate electrode, the electrons trapped in the quantum well of GQDs are emitted back to the p-Si. These events are illustrated schematically in Fig. 7 . This phenomenon is due to the size effect of GQDs and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups at their edges (the technical data sheet from ACS Materials shows the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the GQDs [34]). These functional groups potentially play roles in quantum confinement and this leads to discrete quantum states in the GQDs. The functionalized edge states originating from the oxygen-containing functional groups help to form multiple energy levels, which facilitate greater charge trapping in GQDs. Moreover, these functional groups add structural defects that serve as additional charge trapping centers in the GQD domain.

To investigate the charge retention properties, the GQD-embedded Si devices were placed under constant voltage stressing of ±12 V for 2 s. Fig. 8 shows the charge retention property and flat-band voltage shift of the memory device. The flat band voltage was shifted by 12.9 mV even after 105 s and this low voltage change over time confirmed the very low generation of traps at the interface, which is an important condition for dielectric stability. In addition, a charge loss of only 14.9% was observed after 105 s, thereby indicating the excellent charge confinement property of the GQDs, which acted as the trapping centers for electrons.

A shown in Fig. 9 , we examined the reliability and stability of the NVM devices based on their endurance characteristics while applying a gate voltage of ±12 V. Fig. 9 shows the P/E cyclic characteristics of the memory capacitors. The flat band voltage shifts were only 0.16 V and 0.23 V after 104 cycles during the programming and erasing operations, respectively, which confirmed the reliability and stable operation of the devices. The programmed flat band increased gradually, which can be explained by the fact that during programming, only a portion of the electrons that were not captured by the GQDs were either trapped in the control oxide or reached the control gate. The nominal increased shift in the flat band voltage during erasing can be attributed to the incomplete removal of electrons from the control oxide. The conduction mechanism via F–N tunneling is consistent with that reported in previous studies [28]. The memory behavior, charge retention, and endurance properties were also comparable to or even better than those reported previously [25,28,29].

In this study, we investigated the memory characteristics of GQD-based NVM devices prepared using PMMA as a tunneling and control dielectric material. The average size of the GQDs was determined based on scanning electron microscopy images. A thin film of GQDs measuring 35 nm was grown between the tunneling and control oxide by spin coating, which was confirmed based on a cross-sectional TEM image. Our analysis of the transport mechanism showed the dominance of F–N tunneling in the high electric field regions (1.4–1.6 MV/cm). The memory characteristics were obtained based on C–V measurements. A maximum memory window of 5.54 V was obtained after applying ±7 V to the gate electrode. A control experiment was performed to confirm the validity of GQD as a charge trap element and very low hysteresis (∼7 mV) was found in the control device. The charge retention properties were also assessed and the charge loss was only 14.9% from the NVM devices after 105 s. The endurance characteristics were determined according to the flat band voltage shifts, which were only 0.16 V and 0.23 V during the programming and erasing operations, respectively. Thus, our results suggest that GQDs are promising charge trap elements for use in NVM applications. We also found that the device could be fabricated using a solution processing technique, thereby overcoming the need for a highly expensive temperature fabrication process to facilitate cost-effective manufacturing during large-scale production.
